content.problemStatement:
Vishal loves running. He often visits his favourite Nehru Park and runs for very long distances. On one such visit he found that the number of girls in the park was unusually high. Now he wants to use this as an opportunity to impress a large number of girls with his awesome speed.

The track on which he runs is an N\text{N}N kilometres long straight path. There are ai\mathbf{a_i}ai​ girls standing within the ith\text{ith}ith kilometre of this path. A girl will be impressed only if Vishal is running at his maximum speed when he passes by her. But he can run at his best speed only for a single continuous stretch of K\text{K}K kilometres. Now Vishal wants to know what is the maximum number of girls that he can impress.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two space‑separated integers N and K (1 ≤ K ≤ N), where N is the length of the track in kilometres. The second line contains N space‑separated integers a1, a2, …, aN, where ai denotes the number of girls standing in the i‑th kilometre.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible number of girls Vishal can impress by running at his maximum speed for exactly K consecutive kilometres.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The best 2‑kilometre segment is kilometres 2‑3 (4+8), giving 12 impressed girls.
The segment kilometres 3‑5 (3+4+5) yields the maximum sum 12.
The last 5 kilometres (6+7+8+9+10) give the largest possible sum 40.

solution.approach: The problem asks for the maximum sum of any contiguous sub‑array of length exactly K. This is a classic sliding‑window problem. Compute the sum of the first K elements, store it as the current and best sum. Then slide the window one position to the right: subtract the element leaving the window and add the new element entering it. Update the best sum if the new window sum is larger. Repeat until the end of the array. The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
